message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Introduction</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Emerging evidence suggests association of air pollution exposure with risk of SARS-CoV-2 infection, but many of these findings are limited by study design, lack of individual-level covariate data or are specific to certain subpopulations. We aim to evaluate causal effects of air pollution on risk of infection, whilst overcoming these limitations.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Methods</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Concentrations for black carbon(BC), particulate matter 10(PM
                    <jats:sub>10</jats:sub>
                    ), particulate matter 2.5(PM
                    <jats:sub>2.5</jats:sub>
                    ), nitrogen dioxide(NO
                    <jats:sub>2</jats:sub>
                    ) and oxides of nitrogen(NO
                    <jats:sub>x</jats:sub>
                    ) from the Department of Environment, Food and Rural Affairs (DEFRA) and Effect of Low-level Air Pollution: A Study in Europe (ELAPSE) were linked to postcodes of 53,683 Virus Watch study participants. The primary outcome was first SARS-CoV-2 infection, between 1st September 2020 and 30th April 2021. Regression analysis used modified Poisson with robust estimates, clustered by household, adjusting for individual (e.g., age, sex, ethnicity) and environmental covariates(e.g., population density, region) to estimate total and direct effects.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Single pollutant analysis showed the direct effect of higher risk of SARS-CoV-2 infection with increased exposure to PM
                    <jats:sub>2.5</jats:sub>
                    (RR1.11,95%CI 1.08;1.15), PM
                    <jats:sub>10</jats:sub>
                    (RR1.06,95%CI 1.04;1.09), NO
                    <jats:sub>2</jats:sub>
                    (RR1.04,95%CI 1.04;1.05) and NO
                    <jats:sub>x</jats:sub>
                    (RR1.02,95%CI 1.02;1.02) per 1µg/m
                    <jats:sup>3</jats:sup>
                    increment with DEFRA 2015-19 data. Sensitivity analyses altering covariates, exposure window and modelled air pollution data source produced similar estimates. Higher risk of SARS-CoV-2 per 10
                    <jats:sup>-5</jats:sup>
                    m
                    <jats:sup>-1</jats:sup>
                    increment of BC (RR1.86, 95%CI 1.62;2.14) was observed using ELAPSE data.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusion</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Long term exposure to higher concentrations of air pollutions increases the risk of SARS-CoV-2 infection, highlighting that adverse health effects of air pollution is not only limited to non-communicable diseases.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: Assessing the causal effect of air pollution on risk of SARS-CoV-2 infection
